data.title: Missed Meta In 2023? This Might Be Your Second Chance
data.description:
I think the market’s got Meta (NASDAQ:META) wrong right now. The stock’s been knocked down nearly 30% over the past month and while some of that’s tied to broader tech volatility and macro headlines, the core business hasn’t skipped a beat. If anything, it’s firing on more cylinders than ever. Free cash flow hit $52 billion last year, the ad engine is showing real pricing power again, and Meta’s AI and infrastructure bets are scaling fast. But the stock’s trading like it’s stuck in neutral.

At just over $500 a share, Meta is sitting around 20x trailing earnings. That’s low for a company with this level of profitability, balance sheet strength and long-term upside. I don’t think this is just a case of the stock being undervalued—it’s more like the market’s stuck on the wrong narrative. Everyone’s still hung up on Reality Labs losses and regulatory overhangs, but meanwhile, WhatsApp, Threads and Meta AI are slowly becoming real businesses. It’s a disconnect. And for long-term investors, that’s where the edge is.

Why the Market’s Getting This Wrong

Let’s be real—some of the skepticism around Meta isn’t totally crazy. Reality Labs lost $17.7 billion last year and Zuckerberg’s still pouring billions into VR and AR while the payoff feels miles away. But what the market seems to be missing is how insanely strong the Family of Apps business still is. $87 billion in operating income on a 54% margin. That’s not “stable” or “mature”—that’s world-class efficiency.

Even more important: ad impressions are still rising (up 11% in 2024) but now average ad pricing is growing again too (up 10%). That means Meta’s AI-driven ad targeting is working. And it’s happening without the usual reliance on third-party data. The company is figuring out how to make money in a post-ATT privacy-conscious world. That’s huge.

On top of that WhatsApp Business revenue jumped 63% year-over-year. Threads which most people still write off is slowly gaining traction. These aren’t side projects anymore—they’re potential multi-billion-dollar platforms. But the market still treats Meta like a one-trick pony with a high cash burn side hustle.

What the Company’s Actually Saying

Digging into the 10-K management’s message is pretty consistent: we’re not backing off Reality Labs but we’re also not ignoring near-term results. Capex hit $39 billion last year, and they’re guiding $60–$65 billion for 2025—mostly to scale AI infrastructure. That sounds aggressive and it is. But when you’re building your own compute stack models and user endpoints, that spend starts to make sense.

They also called out Meta AI and Llama models more directly this time. These are no longer research toys—they’re being embedded across Instagram, WhatsApp and hardware like smart glasses. If Meta nails this, it could end up with one of the few vertically integrated AI platforms that touches billions of users.

And don’t forget: Meta’s not just spending big—it’s returning capital too. $29.75 billion in buybacks last year and a brand-new dividend. That’s a company confident in its cash flow, not one scrambling to stay relevant.

Valuation Looks Way Too Conservative

This is where things really start to jump out. Meta trades at around 20x trailing earnings despite putting up 38% net margins and nearly $165 billion in revenue last year. EV/EBITDA is hovering around 15x. Compare that to peers: Microsoft’s (MSFT) around 30 earnings, and Amazon (AMZN) is north of 30x. Meta looks underpriced, plain and simple.

If you assume Meta keeps growing the core ad business at a steady 10%, Reality Labs starts to lose less money and free cash flow climbs into the $60–65 range by 2027—which doesn't seem far-fetched given they just did $52 billion last year—you don’t need a crazy multiple to get to $650–$750 per share. That’s 30–50% upside from here. And that doesn’t even bake in a breakout from Threads or a real monetization curve on Meta’s AI tools.

What the Headlines Are Missing

Lately the broader mood around tech has turned sour. You can see it in the headlines: recession talk is back, oil prices are sliding, and Trump’s tariff drama has global markets on edge. Currency volatility in China and a dip in the dollar are just adding fuel to the fire. Everyone’s nervous. And when markets get nervous they start unloading anything with long-dated payoffs—like Meta.

But here’s the thing: none of this has much to do with Meta’s fundamentals. If anything, the company’s insulated by its massive user base and durable ad platform. The news sentiment around Meta specifically? Mostly neutral. Some lawsuits, some product chatter but nothing damaging. Yet the stock’s acting like there was a major miss or guidance cut. There wasn’t.

And while the headlines are screaming about tariff chaos, Meta is launching new versions of its smart glasses at the $1000 price point and scaling AI use across its product suite. That’s not a company pulling back. It’s one that’s doubling down on platform shifts.

Risks Worth Watching

Meta’s got no shortage of risk—some of it baked into the business model, some of it self-inflicted, and a lot of it coming from the outside world. The biggest and most immediate is regulation. Europe, in particular, is hitting hard. The company’s already dealt with a €1.2 billion GDPR fine and had to change how it handles ad targeting across the EU. Between the Digital Markets Act, AI regulation and growing pressure in the U.S., there’s a real chance Meta’s core ad engine gets clipped in key markets.

Then there’s Reality Labs, which is still bleeding cash—$17.7 billion in losses last year alone. Meta keeps insisting this is a long-term play on spatial computing and maybe it is, but there’s no guarantee the bet pays off. A lot is riding on hardware like its upcoming smart glasses. If those flop, it just adds to the skepticism around the entire division.

Content and platform safety are another ongoing battle. Meta’s platforms are massive and messy. There’s no real way to guarantee everything’s clean. The company continues to deal with misinformation, scams, election interference and illegal content slipping through. And now that it’s easing up on content moderation in the name of free expression, that could open it up to new legal headaches.

Cybersecurity is a quieter risk, but no less serious. Meta admits it’s a regular target and that not every hole has been patched. On top of that, it relies on a web of partners and vendors who could become weak links in the chain.

Add in the talent war for AI engineers, exposure to foreign governments, taxes, currency swings and the occasional threat of outright bans—and yeah, there’s a lot going on. But even with all of that, Meta’s still pumping out over $50 billion in free cash flow. The question is whether the market’s overreacting to the noise. Right now, it looks like it might be.

How This Could Play Out

Bear case: In a bear case, Reality Labs keeps bleeding cash with no visible payoff, Threads stays flat or fails and regulators—especially in the EU—keep coming after Meta’s core business. Throw in some macro drag (maybe ad budgets tighten) and earnings miss the low end of estimates. Say EPS drops to around $23. If the market’s feeling sour and only gives that 18x, you’re looking at a stock in the $400–$410 range. Not great, but not a total disaster either—especially considering how much cash Meta still throws off.

Base case: Now, let’s say nothing crazy happens—no wild upside but no disasters either. Ads keep growing at a healthy pace, WhatsApp and Threads add some incremental revenue and AI tools improve margins a bit. Reality Labs continues to lose money but it doesn’t spiral. EPS hits around $29 by 2026, as consensus suggests. Give that a modest 22–25x multiple (not a stretch for a company with 38% net margins), and you’re looking at a price somewhere in the $640–$725 range. That’s a solid return from where the stock sits today.

Bull case: Now here’s where it could really get interesting. If Meta starts unlocking real monetization on Threads, if WhatsApp starts printing meaningful revenue and if Meta AI tools catch on in a meaningful way across both consumer and business apps, then suddenly you’re looking at earnings that blow past current estimates. EPS could push past $35. And if the market rewards that with a 30x multiple? That puts the stock somewhere north of $1,000. That’s a stretch, sure—but it’s not some fantasy moonshot either. Meta has the infrastructure and reach to pull this off if things click.

The base case feels very realistic. And the upside is hard to ignore.

Bottom Line

Meta’s not perfect. It’s spending big, it's taking long-term bets, and it’s facing plenty of heat from regulators and the media. But strip away the noise and you’re left with a company making more money than ever with more levers for growth than it’s had in years—and a stock price that doesn’t seem to reflect any of it.

The last time Meta was this cheap was late 2022, right before it ripped higher. This feels similar. The stock is down not because the business is breaking—but because investors are tired of the story.

That’s usually when things start to get interesting.

My take? Meta’s a Strong Buy. The numbers back it up. The strategy is working. And the market’s not paying attention. That’s exactly when you want to lean in.

data.title: InterDigital: Major Licensing Milestones, But Very Expensive (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
​​InterDigital, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDCC) is a tech company that mainly develops wireless, video, and artificial intelligence [AI] foundational knowledge, standards, and devices. Their work in Internet-of-Things [IoT], edge computing, and optimization has also helped lay the foundation for solutions used in smart cities, homes, and industries. And overall, it’s a fantastic business, but unfortunately, it seems its stock has already priced in a sizeable premium. And, given the current odds of a recession, I feel expensive stocks like IDCC are particularly exposed if there’s a market-wide repricing to lower valuation multiples. So, on balance, I now feel IDCC is a “Hold” for now.

Tech Services And Devices

​InterDigital, Inc. specializes in wireless, video, and artificial intelligence [AI] technologies to produce innovative services and devices. IDCC was founded back in 1972, and it’s currently headquartered in Delaware, USA. As previously noted, their business segments focus on wireless, video, and AI, broadly speaking. The wireless segment has made roughly 4,000 contributions to foundational international standards and has contributed to innovations across several architectures. They’re now focusing on next-generation wireless technologies, such as 5G and 6G. Since my previous article from February 2024, IDCC has made some interesting progress with its IP portfolio, but the stock now does look expensive relative to its potential.

You see, IDCC’s video lab collaborates with other industry bodies to develop video standards, computer graphics, and streaming. Overall, IDCC actually plays a relatively important role in defining and implementing MPEG Immersive Video [MIV] and Extended Reality [XR] for interactive environments. Similarly, their AI lab works on topics that are transversal to wireless and video. Here, IDCC essentially develops solutions that optimize 5G and beyond, which often includes designing video codecs using deep learning algorithms.

IDCC also has some offerings with Internet-of-Things [IoT] devices and technologies. It has contributed to developing core platforms for its connectivity. These systems include 1) low-power communication protocols to allow IoT sensors to run for a long time with small batteries.

Also, they have 2) edge computing for the data to be processed in the device, reducing latency, and 3) dynamic connectivity for 5G to prioritize resources according to their needs. Interestingly, their integration of AI plus IoT can be applied to smart cities. In practical terms, this eventually involves automated control systems that can be used for traffic administration, lighting, and waste management.

This is why IDCC’s contributions are immensely beneficial for smart homes with sensors and appliances so that they can interact with humans. Lastly, the merging of AI and IoT will probably have a great impact on the industrial sector by generating predictive maintenance, with machines automatically reporting issues as they pop up. So all of these factors essentially place IDCC at the foundation of major tech trends.

Major Tech Partnerships

But, more recently, in Q4 2024, IDCC secured several important milestones that give it some interesting strategic momentum. The company reached a new deal with the Oppo Group. The deal basically states that they will use IDCC’s patented wireless, video, and AI technologies.

And it’s worth mentioning that Oppo is one of the largest producers of smartphones in China, with brands like realme and OnePlus. So, with this collaboration, IDCC now has license agreements with the top four cellular phone manufacturers. In other words, they’re now embedded with Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF), and now Oppo. Thus, this basically gives IDCC 70% market coverage in its niche.

Moreover, before this particular IDCC deal with Oppo. These two companies were actually engaged in multiple patent infringement litigations in countries like Germany, the UK, India, and China. This contentious relationship was mostly centered on IDCC’s standard-essential patents [SEPs] related to 3G, 4G, and 5G technologies. So, this agreement also gives IDCC a legal win because these claims are considered dismissed going forward. It basically managed to turn a foe into a friend, which, I think, is, by itself, quite brilliant from management.

Additionally, IDCC renewed its license agreement with ZTE, which is a major telecom and smartphone firm. They had previously signed a deal in 2019 resolving a nine-year patent dispute. And in their latest corporate presentation, IDCC confirmed they renewed this license agreement. It’s also worth mentioning that IDCC also signed the resolution of conflicts with Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) by agreeing to a binding arbitration. This particular arbitration concluded six years of litigation that coincidentally started back in 2019 when IDCC sued Lenovo in the UK High Court for infringement of patents related to 3G and 4G standards.

But probably one of their most important deals happened this week. In April 07, 2025, IDCC announced a multi-year license agreement with HP Inc. (HPQ). This latest deal basically lets HPQ use IDCC’s technologies in its personal computers. Concretely, HP will now be able to incorporate IDCC’s wi-fi and video decoding solutions. And for IDCC, this deal grows its influence in the PC sector, reaching coverage of 50% of the PC global market.

Besides, I believe this only foreshadows even more growth across industries over time, because clearly, IDCC’s technologies are at the core of many potential tech implementations. And on top of that, IDCC recently received the Fierce Network Innovation Award for AI for wireless communications and its contributions to global standards. So, I would say they probably have among the best reputations and competitive edges in this particular industry.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, IDCC trades at a $5.0 billion market cap, making it already one of the largest companies in its niche. Here’s where I feel it’s now time to start being cautious on IDCC. Its balance sheet holds roughly $527.4 million in cash and $430.9 million in short-term investments. This amounts to about $958.3 million in liquidity that they can deploy for their operations, against essentially $471.8 million in debt.

But a quick note on this, $456.3 million of its debt is due within 12 months, so I think a better proxy for their actual liquidity is $486.5 million (assuming they don’t refinance). Lastly, their book value stands at $857.2 million, which does indicate an expensive P/B of 5.8. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is much lower at just 2.7.

Likewise, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on IDCC, they’re projected to generate just $679.5 million in revenues this year. That would also price them at a high P/S of 7.4. Again, their peers’ P/S is much lower at 2.2. In fact, back in my original February 2024 IDCC article, I said my “bull” case for the stock was $203.53 per share. This is why I feel that, at today’s prices, IDCC seems to have already played out its upside potential, as I initially outlined.

So, I think it’s fair to say IDCC is already priced for success, which, I believe, is probably the main drawback with this stock. Other than that, IDCC is actually a cash flow machine at the moment. I estimate they generate in Q4 2024 alone approximately $188.1 million. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX. If we annualize that figure, it would imply IDCC produces almost $752.4 million in positive cash flow every year, which I think is probably its most impressive metric.

But, I still believe that if the economy enters into a recession, stocks that trade at a premium will probably suffer the most. For context, JPMorgan (JPM) maintains a 60% chance of recession despite the pause in the tariffs, so this is no longer an unlikely scenario. And in that sense, IDCC has so far fared relatively well during this latest market pullback, dropping by just 15% so far. Yet, if markets keep dropping, I feel IDCC in particular has much more downside risk at these levels.

Hence, I feel that despite its strong underlying business, the stock itself is probably a “Hold” at these levels for now. Additionally, while they’ve scored some legal wins recently, it’s always possible that some other company might litigate them again. So I do think IDCC investors are still exposed to that type of headline risk, which is definitely heightened due to its currently expensive valuation.

Conclusion: Cautious “Hold” For Now

Overall, I think IDCC has a fantastic business, and it has recently made some important partnerships that only grow its influence across several industries. They’re also a cash flow machine, so I don’t think they’ll have any financing problems for the foreseeable future, and their balance sheet is solid. However, it seems their stock is already a bit too pricey, in particular if the macro environment remains challenging. And if a recession hits, I believe stocks that have a lot of premium embedded into them will probably get hit the worst. Hence, I think on balance, a “Hold” rating makes sense for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Tariff Rollercoaster Continues, ECB Rate Decision And Inflation Data In Focus
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: US Dollar on the ropes following topsy-turvy week

It's been a wild ride to say the least for financial markets this week. Wild swings, multi-year highs and lows being tested, tariffs being pulled back and of course the escalating tensions between the US and China.

The developments this week necessitate us starting with FX markets as the US dollar endured heavy losses, hitting 2022 lows in the process.

The FX market tells an interesting story. Among G10 currencies, only the less-traded Norwegian krone hasn’t moved against the dollar since last Friday. Other currencies have gained, with the pound up 0.8%, the euro, Australian dollar and New Zealand dollar rising 2-3%, and the Swiss franc leading with a 4.5% increase.

Markets are clearly telling us that there is a crisis of confidence in the US dollar at the moment. Whether I believe there should be or not is an entirely different and irrelevant discussion given what we have already seen from markets.

On Thursday, we saw investors move away from US assets. Both stocks and Treasury bonds dropped, even though core inflation came in much lower than expected (0.1% instead of the predicted 0.3% for the month).

Markets are definitely telling us that data is not the main driving force behind market moves. This honor is taken by concern about the combined threat of inflation and growth deceleration.

A perfect example of the challenges facing global markets is reflected in the losses for stock markets around the world. More than $5 trillion in market value has vanished from the MSCI all-country index of world stocks during the roller-coaster ride since April 2.

These developments have helped Gold prices once more, with the precious metal making multiple fresh highs this week. Gold peaked on Friday at around $3245/oz at the time of writing. The precious metal is on course to finish the week around 6.5% up

Oil prices rose on Friday, but both Brent and WTI are set for a second consecutive week of losses. Tariffs being the instigator here as well as recessionary fears are impacting oil demand forecasts just as OPEC + increased output. Earlier today, President Trump's administration said the President wants the US to pump more and more oil, further exacerbating demand and supply dynamics.

US Equity Indexes finished the week on a positive note, with the Dow Jones increasing by 648.65 points (1.64%) to 40,242.31. The S&P 500 went up 93.70 points (1.78%) to 5,361.75, and the Nasdaq climbed 312.92 points (1.91%) to 16,700.23. The week was a positive one for US stocks following President Trump's pause on his 'liberation day tariff agenda'. The move has seen US indexes recover most of the losses which occurred since the April 2 announcement.

The biggest story this week aside from the US dollar comes from the US Treasury market. U.S. 10-year Treasury yields saw their biggest weekly jump in over 20 years on Friday. Rumors of selling, or lack of buying, by foreign investors are adding to concerns over the market with rumors that hedge funds and asset managers were getting margin calls.

Another reason cited for the performance comes through the unwinding of basis trades, a strategy where investors earn from the gap between cash Treasury prices and futures. Many believe this is a large factor behind this week's volatility.

An interesting take from ING THINK caught my attention this week. To summarize, the piece opined that U.S. tariff policies haven’t changed much overall. Here’s why:

Before Trump took office, the average U.S. tariff on imports was 2.5%. By Wednesday morning, with actions like 25% tariffs on metals and autos, and up to 50% on China, that average rose to 28%—the highest since the early 20th century.

Midweek, some countries like the EU and parts of Southeast Asia saw their tariffs drop to 10%. However, China's tariffs, already high at 104%, jumped to 145%.

These adjustments balanced out, leaving the average tariff rate unchanged at 28%.

**Only non-USMCA compliant goods from Canada and Mexico are subject to a 25% tariff. Assumes latest China tariff of 145%, as per latest Bloomberg reports. Colored bars show the contribution to the latest average tariff rate from the tariffs charged on different countries and products.

I found this to be an interesting take and something I had partly anticipated having paid attention to President Trump's previous term. However, it does not hold much weight in the current climate as markets are still moving and that is the reason why we are here after all.

The week ahead: Shortened trading week brings a host of data releases

The upcoming week will focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's plans for new tariffs. Alongside this, markets will also watch U.S. jobs data, an Australian central bank meeting, and a key eurozone inflation report.

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region will be Japanese inflation and a host of data releases from China.

In Japan, inflation is expected to rise, but the Bank of Japan (BoJ) is likely to keep its rate cuts on hold. Goods being shipped early to the US are expected to boost machinery orders and exports.

However, this positive trend might not last beyond the first quarter due to tariff uncertainties. Consumer prices are predicted to go up in March, mainly due to higher service costs. Earlier fresh food price hikes are also expected to show up in eating-out and processed food costs. The BoJ will keep a close eye on how US tariff policies affect the economy while maintaining current rates for now.

It's a busy data week in China as the country grapples with the escalating trade war with the US. None of the tariffs so far are expected to show any impact on the data yet but should do so moving forward.

China will release key data for March, including first quarter GDP, on Thursday. A 5.3% year-on-year growth is expected, though this is before tariffs start impacting from the second quarter. For now, domestic activity seems steady, with retail sales and industrial production likely to grow by 4.5% and 6.0%, respectively. Fixed-asset investment is expected to stay mostly stable at 4.0%. Trade is slowing as tariffs take effect, but bigger impacts may come later.

Property prices will also be watched to see if market volatility has pushed them down further. Supporting domestic demand is the focus this year, though falling home prices make it hard to boost household confidence. While prices in major cities have stabilized, it’s unclear if this is the bottom.

Meanwhile, all attention is on how China will respond to the 145% tariffs imposed by Trump with a 125% tariff on US goods due to start on April 12. The market expects possible rate cuts, lower reserve requirement ratios (RRR), and new fiscal policies to support domestic demand.

In the Pacific region, we have some high-impact data from both Australia and New Zealand. On Tuesday, we have the RBA minutes, followed by Wednesday's New Zealand CPI release. The week ends on Thursday with employment data out of Australia.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US is enjoying a bit of a data break next week, but the tariff conversations will remain at the forefront. The eurozone and the UK have a bit more to offer on the data front next week, with the ECB interest rate meeting taking center stage.

The US is expected to have a calmer week due to Easter, but challenges remain. Tariffs have been delayed by 90 days, yet price hikes are still looming. Households are dealing with three key issues: higher prices reducing spending power, job loss fears due to federal budget cuts, and stock market drops making people cautious about major purchases.

March retail sales are likely to show strong growth as consumers rushed to buy before tariffs take effect. Auto sales rose 10.6%, and credit card data points to high demand for electronics and appliances. This may keep GDP growth positive in the first quarter, but future weakness is a concern.

Industrial output will be under the spotlight as Trump pushes for reshoring. However, unclear trade policies and fears of retaliation are making businesses nervous. Meanwhile, the Fed is expected to cut rates by around 90 basis points this year, with three or four 25bp cuts looking likely.

The rise in employers' national insurance could slow the job market, but so far, redundancy data hasn't increased. Watch for any drop in job vacancies or payroll numbers, though it seems unlikely.

Lower petrol prices may slightly reduce overall inflation in March, but services inflation is unlikely to fall much yet. More improvement could come in the second quarter if annual price adjustments are less steep. This is why the Bank of England may continue cutting rates each quarter through 2025 and into 2026.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

This week's focus remains on the US Dollar Index as it looks to recover from levels last seen in 2022.

The DXY has pushed below the psychological 100 barrier as well for the first time since July 2023. The last time the DXY breached this level, the move proved short-lived with the DXY going on an extended rally to the upside thereafter.

Given the dynamics at play this time around, such a move sees unlikely. However, a push back above the 100.00 barrier cannot be ruled out.

Given the speed of the selloff this week, coupled with the RSI period-14 hovering in oversold territory and the downside wick on Friday, a bounce cannot be ruled out.

Immediate resistance rests at 100.00 before the 100.617 and 101.18 handles come into focus.

If the selloff continues next week, immediate support rests at 99.57 and 99.00. A break of the 90.00 mark could result in a swift move lower toward the 97.70 support handle.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart - April 11, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 99.57
  • 99.00
  • 97.70

Resistance

  • 100.00
  • 100.61
  • 101.18

data.title: TQQQ: A Contrarian Mag 7 ETF Buy
data.description:
The ProShares UltraPro QQQ (NASDAQ:TQQQ) has experienced a double-digit valuation correction this year as markets have experienced significant declines by concerns over an escalating trade war. In my opinion, the drop in valuations, especially in the big tech group, is a buying opportunity for investors that want to start to build long-term exposure to the fast-growing technology market which is being fueled by unprecedented gains in artificial intelligence. With AI adoption only going to rise going forward and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) reporting massive revenue growth in Q1'25, I believe the drop in the tech sector creates a great entry point for long-term investors into the ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

Previous rating

I rated the ProShares UltraPro QQQ a buy in my previous work on the ETF -- A Leveraged ETF Bet On Mag 7 Stocks -- because it was heavily over-weight ‘magnificent 7’ stocks that were set to benefit, in the long term, from growing investments in AI technology. With tech stocks really getting punished in the last several weeks, I believe that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a highly promising investment on the drop.

Leveraged bet on cheap magnificent 7 stocks

ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a leveraged bet on the tech sector as the ETF seeks to 3X the daily performance of its underlying benchmark, the Nasdaq-100 index. The recent market turmoil, related to the U.S. announcing new tariffs last week, has made leading tech franchises massively cheaper, with companies like Alphabet (GOOG) trading at just 16X forward (FY 2026) earnings.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ’s portfolio is dominated by magnificent 7 stocks, which are the seven most influential and powerful technology firms listed on U.S. stock exchanges. This group includes Alphabet, Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT).

The highest weighting in the ProShares UltraPro QQQ goes to Microsoft with a representation of 7.4%, followed by Apple (7.2%) and Nvidia (6.6%). Google is included in the top ten investments with a share of 3.4% because of TQQQ’s investment in Google Class A shares. However, the ETF also owns Google Class C shares (not included in the top ten holdings) which brings the ETF’s combined Alphabet exposure to 3.7%. In total, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ's top ten holdings represented 42.6% of the ETF's investments, which amounted to $24.8B on a net asset value basis.

All magnificent 7 stocks have material exposure to the theme of artificial intelligence, which has already been dominating investors' minds for at least the last 2–3 years. With AI adoption set to rise going forward, TQQQ's leading portfolio holdings (the top ten constituents broken down above) are set for sustained earnings and free cash flow growth.

Chipmaker TSMC just released preliminary Q1'25 revenue, which surged 42% compared to the year-earlier period due to a continual AI boom in the (server) market. Additionally, Gartner recently made a prediction that called for a massive 76% year-over-year increase in AI spending FY 2025, fueled by generative AI services.

In addition, the temporary suspension of tariffs, announced only a week ago, has led to a massive rebound in technology stocks on Wednesday. Since magnificent 7 stocks especially has seen dramatic valuation draw-downs recently, I believe that an entry into the technology sector could make a lot of sense, especially when considering that 1) Tech stocks are still quite cheap, and 2) The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has delivered impressive annual NAV returns since inception.

Impressive annual returns and long term growth potential

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ is a leveraged bet on magnificent 7 stocks and because of the strong performance of U.S. large-cap tech companies in the last decade, TQQQ has an impressive return profile: the ETF has achieved an annual NAV return of 38.83% since inception (the ProShares UltraPro QQQ was launched on February 9, 2010). With AI being a multi-year investment theme, I believe the current correction in the tech market is a unique opportunity to build exposure to ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

TQQQ’s valuation

Tech stocks have collapsed in the last several days, before benefiting from a massive rally on Wednesday after it was announced that the U.S. president paused previously announced tariffs for 90 days (with the exception of China). The Nasdaq-100, the underlying benchmark of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, bounced back especially hard on Wednesday, gaining 10%.

This rebound, in my opinion, is going to continue considering how cheap magnificent 7 stocks have become. If the U.S. reaches more trade deals with other countries, negative sentiment overhang could dissipate and clear the route for a new up-leg in the tech sector.

All magnificent 7 stocks, as an example, are still very much expected to see double-digit earnings growth in the years ahead and are trading at forward P/E ratios between 15-25X... which is bargain territory, in my opinion. Google, as an example, is trading at only 16.0X forward earnings which calculates to a 6%+ earnings yield and the company is expected to grow its earnings 16% annually in the long term. Google is one of the best deals in the market right now, in my opinion: Why I Am Going All-In Now.

It should be noted that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ employs leverage which has resulted, given the long term out-performance of large-cap U.S. technology companies, to highly impressive annual NAV returns. Since its inception, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has increased its net asset value by 2,230%, out-matching other leveraged ETF investment options like the Direxion Daily Technology Bull 3x Shares (TECL) by a massive margin.

Risks with TQQQ

I see two major risks for the ProShares UltraPro QQQ: 1) The ETF uses leverage to magnify its returns. Therefore, in a down-market, and especially in a market that punishes technology companies, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has a material chance to seriously under-perform the market, 2) The ETF is a sector-specific investment which investor are going to miss out on any kind of upside related to non-tech sectors going forward.

Final thoughts

The drop in valuations in the U.S. tech sector has led to a massive decline in the value of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF: the fund has dropped from $90 in February to less than $40 this week, before bouncing back 35% on Wednesday to $52. The year-to-date return is also looking weak: TQQQ is down 33% so far in FY 2025 which reflects the risks of a leveraged, magnificent 7-focused ETF. I believe, however, given that AI trends in the market are long term and that TSMC's Q1'25 revenue update indicates that the AI boom is not slowing down, that the ProShares UltraPro QQQ has massive rebound potential. In my opinion, the drop constitutes a very interesting engagement opportunity and I see a highly favorable risk profile for long term investors that understand the risks and are capable of dealing with a high amount of volatility.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed-Income Monitor: April 2025
data.description:
By Katherine Nuss, CFA

Our proprietary Fixed-Income Monitor compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income. It’s designed to help investors identify opportunities and risks in the asset class.

Key takeaways for April 2025

  • Rising uncertainty related to escalating trade tensions fueled investor anxiety during March, sending risky assets lower in a flight to quality. High-quality fixed income is working as an effective risk hedge, with U.S. Treasury prices positive for the month and up over equities by more than 6% year to date.
  • Multiple measures of consumer and business sentiment collapsed during the month, highlighting the potential risks to economic growth from the implementation of broad-based tariffs. As a result, credit spreads in the most economically sensitive segments of the bond market widened. While this has created somewhat more attractive valuations, spreads remain tight to levels established during the growth scare of August 2024.
  • Municipal bonds lagged both Treasuries and investment-grade corporate bonds on challenging season patterns and the above-mentioned policy uncertainties.

Interpreting yields and spreads

One way to identify opportunities in fixed income is to look at bond yields. That’s because yield, which is based on a bond's price and coupon payments, reflects total return potential. Yields can change over time and across bond sectors. Spread, which refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries),1 indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk. If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are being paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less.

Disclosures

1 There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. data.title: ANGL: Higher Quality Outperforms Amid Volatility And Spike In Downgrades
data.description:
Fallen angels ended Q1 strong, outpacing rising stars with a 21% market value gain and driving a notable shift in market composition.

Fallen angels (as represented by the ICE US Fallen Angel High Yield 10% Constrained Index, "H0CF") finished Q1 on a strong note, outperforming the broad high-yield market (as represented by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, "H0A0") by 0.60% (1.55% vs. 0.95%). Increasing concern around U.S. trade policy and the expected negative impacts on economic growth and inflation drove high yield credit spreads to their highest levels since August 2024. Spreads have continued to widen in the first few days of April, very close to the October 2023 levels (high yield spreads ~450bps). Higher quality credit outperformed in this more uncertain environment, benefitting fallen angels relative to the broad market, given their approximately 26% overweight to BB rated bonds and 6% underweight to CCC and below rated bonds. CCCs are now down -0.67% YTD after also underperforming in February, while higher quality, BB-rated bonds outperformed each month in the quarter at 1.29%, 0.72% and -0.56% respectively. Despite strong balance sheets and fundamentals overall, spread levels remain historically tight even after this recent widening, making high yield (especially lower rated bonds) vulnerable to continued price declines as the market digests the impact of tariffs and amid generally increased uncertainty. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield fluctuated during the period and ended the quarter at 4.21%, a decline of 0.32%. The yield curve steepened in March, with a higher degree of rate cuts being reflected in the 2-year rate, but 2yr/10yr ended the quarter approximately where it began, and overall longer duration exposure outperformed.

BB- Rated Bonds Continue to Outperform

Fallen Angels Overall Statistics: Fallen angels and broad high yield spreads widened in the first quarter of the year by 8bps and 63bps, respectively, as lower rated bonds exhibited wider spreads, particularly in March, with CCC & below rated bonds widening by 168bps. The widening reflects an escalating trade war and policy uncertainty, along with increased odds of a U.S. economic slowdown and higher inflation. Yields remained relatively high for both indices, although fallen angels finished the quarter slightly lower than the beginning of the year. Broad high yield, on the other hand, ended the quarter with wider spreads offsetting a decline in risk-free yields, due to a 45% exposure to single-B or below-rated bonds. Fallen angel stats were impacted by the addition of new fallen angels equating to approximately 20% of added index weight. The new entrants (Nissan Motor Acceptance, Nissan Motor and Celanese US Holding) entered with lower yields and spreads relative to the overall index.

Index performance is not representative of strategy performance. It is not possible to invest in an index.

Fallen Angels: Aptiv PLC (APTV) junior subordinated debt was downgraded by Fitch to BB+ from BBB- in January, as the company is planning to spin off its electrical distribution systems business into a separate company. Fitch stated that the downgrade aligns more closely with other similarly junior subordinated debt from other issuers. Over the last six months, the junior subordinated debt price decreased to $99.71 from $101.05. Within the Auto sector, S&P affirmed Ford's BBB- rating in early February but revised their outlook to negative as its expansion over the next couple of years appears to be limited due to slower-than-expected progress on cost reduction, high labor costs and increasing pricing pressure. S&P stated that the negative outlook also reflects an increase in the risk of downgrade over the next 12-24 months, which could make Ford a fallen angel again, all else equal, as Moody's credit rating is Ba1 while Fitch is BBB-. In February, Celanese U.S. Holdings was downgraded by Moody's to Ba1 from Baa3, with a negative outlook, following the downgrade by S&P in November to BB+. The downgrade by Moody's was due to concerns over the company's increased debt levels and potential challenges in maintaining strong cash flow amid market uncertainties and reflects apprehensions about Celanese's ability to reduce leverage while navigating fluctuating demand and pricing pressures in the chemical industry. Moody's and Fitch also downgraded Nissan (OTCPK:NSANF) to high yield with a negative outlook in February, due to declining profitability linked to weakening demand for its older vehicle lineup, especially in markets such as the U.S. and China. Moody's also raised concerns about Nissan's ability to effectively execute its restructuring strategy, including workforce reductions and production cuts, amid ongoing global economic uncertainty while Fitch stated that persistently low profitability and a recovery trajectory that has been slower than anticipated.

Despite the concentration in Autos over the past few months, fallen angels have generally been idiosyncratic in nature. We are keeping an eye on signs for increased systemic downgrade activity, given the increased macro uncertainty following the recent tariff announcement.

data.title: KBWY: The High Yield Isn't Sustainable
data.description:
Overview

REITs are still one of my favorite places to obtain a high dividend yield. I want to grow my passive income with the most hands off approach possible, so physical rental properties have slowly become less desirable to me. I hold many high quality REITs and have been searching for the most effective way to consolidate some of these positions. Invesco KBW Premium Yield Equity REIT ETF (NASDAQ:KBWY) operates as an ETF that focuses on a diversified group of REITs while offering a double-digit dividend yield. The high dividend yield of about 10.8% initially caught my attention, but after looking at prior reporting, I am cautious about the sustainability of the dividend.

Looking at the performance over the last five years, we can see that KBWY's price has fallen by about 13.5%. When including all of the distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 29% over the same period. A nice aspect of KBWY is that the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which can be great for the income-seeking investors. However, the dividend payout amounts have trended downward over the last ten years and I do not see a catalyst for growth in the near term.

Despite the recent price decline, I do not believe that KBWY offers an attractive value proposition to investors at this time. The underlying holdings could use some improvement, as it looks like the fund doesn't actually generate enough earnings to support the distribution. Additionally, KBWY remains heavily suppressed during in this higher interest rate environment. The fund has an inception dating back to 2010 and has an expense ratio of 0.35%. So let's start by taking a look at KBWY's underlying strategies and holdings that it utilizes to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, KBWY has assets under management of only $188M that is spread across 30 different holdings. We can see that the largest focus is on diversified REITs making up 25.14% of assets. This is followed by a focus on Health Care REITs and Office REITs, accounting for 17.39% and 17.26% of assets respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up nearly 60% of the entire fund, so we can assume these will have the greatest impact on KBWY's daily price change and total return potential.

Additionally, I am not a fan of the focus on Office REITs, as the demand for these sorts of properties have significantly decreased over the last five year span. A big contributor to this is the growing acceptance to remote working opportunities. There is plenty of data to support that the demand will likely decrease over the next decade.

From a top holdings perspective, Global Net Lease (GNL) is the largest position at 6.98% of assets. This is followed by a 6.15% weight in SITE Centers (SITC) and a 5.41% weight in Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN). Since the top ten holdings make up over 45% of the fund, I would suggest that investors put some time into researching these top holdings since they are likely to have a big influence on the fund's success. Just as a starting point here are some notable callouts for some of the holdings:

  • Brandywine Realty Trust: share price has fallen by more than 75% over the last decade. Currently, has an F-rating in terms of profitability.
  • EPR Properties (EPR): excellent dividend coverage and a growing portfolio of properties. I did previously cover ERP and issued a Buy rating.
  • CTO Realty Growth (CTO): currently has a strong buy rating from Wall Street analysts. There is a potential upside of 31% based on the average price target.
  • Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH): has declined by more than 45% since inception and recently reduced its dividend by 32%.

Ultimately, I think the holdings offer a mixed bag. There are some high quality REITs in combination with some REITs that I consider to be more speculative in nature. Perhaps this approach may be favorable to you, but for me I prefer to have a portfolio of holdings that have proven and consistent track records of growth.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since KBWY's launch, there hasn't really been an extended period of time where the share price has risen. The only exception would be between 2020 and 2021, when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic. An extremely low interest rate meant that real estate companies were able to acquire very cost efficient debt financing, which could be allocated towards different growth initiatives. Lower rates also created an environment where the cost of any floating rate debt held on the balance sheet became cheaper, essentially improving operating margins for many of these businesses. This is why KBWY experienced growth throughout 2021 when rates were low.

The Fed started to aggressively hike interest rates throughout 2022 and 2023 in response to record-breaking inflationary data. As a result, KBWY's price started to decline since the cost of debt was higher, which shrank operating margins and made it more difficult to grow. We still remain in one of the highest interest rate environments over the last decade, which, I believe, will continue to be a vulnerability for KBWY.

Inflation still remains higher than anticipated, so the Fed has adjusted their outlook on rate cuts. There is now only estimated to be one interest rate cut to take place over 2025. Depending on which direction inflation goes, there's even a chance that we see interest rates get increased at some point over 2025. For this reason, I do not believe that KBWY offers an attractive entry point yet.

Similarly, the most updated reporting available in the semi-annual report dated from February 2024. With the exception of 2021, KBWY has experienced net realized losses every single year since inception. Net investment income levels have also declined since its peak in 2020. Therefore, KBWY's NAV has continued to fall over time and now sits at its all-time low. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $29.25
  • 2020: $18.71
  • 2021: $23.91
  • 2022: $22.07
  • 2023: $19.15
  • 2024 (semi-annual): $17.34

A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that the underlying strategy isn't working for KBWY. The NAV decline is also exacerbated by the fact that the fund puts an emphasis on paying out distributions to shareholders. Since the fund isn't actually able to generate enough earnings to support the distribution, the NAV declining at a much faster rate.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1262 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.8%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by 12, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.5144 per share. Referring back to the same report mentioned above, the fund has only been able to generate earnings in excess of this amount once. For reference, here are the annual earnings total:

  • 2019: ($4.65) per share
  • 2020: ($8.26) per share
  • 2021: $6.63 per share
  • 2022: ($0.49) per share
  • 2023: ($1.34) per share
  • 2024 (semi-annual): ($1.03) per share

As a result, the dividend history reveals that the payout amounts have been on a slow decline over the last decade. So despite a generous starting dividend yield, KBWY isn't actually a great choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income. I anticipate the payouts to continue trending downward over time until the interest rate environment becomes more favorable.

Since distributions are funded by net investment income, the dividends received are classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends have the least favorable tax consequences when compared to the qualified dividends that you'd receive from more traditional dividend paying companies. Therefore, a position in KBWY would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am rating KBWY as a sell due to the continuous decline of the NAV and unsustainable dividend coverage as a result of the poor mix of holdings. While there are some solid REITs within KBWY, the ETF includes some less desirable holdings that are more speculative in nature. KBWY has demonstrated a clear vulnerability to the current high-interest rate environment, and the appeal is limited since the market doesn't anticipate interest rate cuts to happen anytime soon.

data.title: QGRO: A Quality Growth ETF That's Winning
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:QGRO) on December 12, 2023, rating it a "buy" after noting the positive impact its May 2023 Index change had on the portfolio's fundamentals. Since that review, QGRO has outperformed most large-cap growth funds, without much help from top mega-gap stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL).

I've read other articles covering QGRO since and was surprised to see other analysts not acknowledge the Index change, instead choosing to rely on its performance since its inception to help justify their conclusions. This article is different. Rather than letting historical price charts cloud my outlook, I'll look at what makes QGRO work from a factor perspective. With this information, I hope I can convince you that QGRO is actually one of the strongest all-around large-cap growth funds on the market, and if you're intent on limiting exposure to the stock market's largest companies, it might be the best one available, period. I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

QGRO Overview

Strategy Overview and Composition

Effective May 31, 2023, QGRO tracks the American Century U.S. Quality Growth Index, which, according to its fact sheet, enhances core growth exposure by:

  • Identifying top growth companies that feature sound fundamentals.
  • Allocating dynamically to stable and high-growth stocks depending on the market environment.
  • Seeking to manage risk through position limits and by emphasizing larger, less volatile companies.

The last point is understated. According to a composition analysis comparing QGRO with the four ETFs listed earlier (IWF, SCHG, VUG, SPYG), QGRO has substantially less exposure to the top 10 holdings common to all funds. As of April 9, 2025, total exposure to these ten stocks is only 16% compared to 57% on average.

Index Methodology Summary

Based on the Index methodology document, updated as of November 2024, I've summarized the following key points of the selection process below.

1. American Century Investment Management is the Index provider and is responsible for constructing two portfolios from the securities in the S-Network US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index, excluding the bottom 2% by market cap: 1) Stable Growth and 2) Pure Growth, with the final portfolio being a blend of these two sub-portfolios.

2. The Index Provider ranks each security by quality and excludes stocks ranked in the bottom 25% in aggregate and in the bottom 25% within their respective sectors. The attributes used are summarized below and are combined to create a quality score for each security.

3. The Index Provider assigns each security a value score based on the attributes below.

3. The Index Provider assigns each security a growth score based on the attributes below and removes the bottom 40% of companies in aggregate.

4. The Index Provider creates a Pure Growth sub-portfolio that maximizes the growth score, subject to the following constraints:

  • Sector: 50-200% of the market sector weight
  • Quality: At least 15% above the market
  • Growth: At least 30% above the market
  • Market Cap: At least 42% of the cumulative market cap

5. The Index Provider creates a Stable Growth sub-portfolio that maximizes the value and profitability composite scores, subject to the following constraints:

  • Sector: 30-150% of the market sector weight
  • Quality: At least 15% above the market
  • Growth: At least 15% above the market
  • Market Cap: At least 42% of the cumulative market cap

6. Each sub-portfolio is subject to security constraints, as follows:

  • Individual Stock Weights: At least 0.25%
  • Size and Volatility: grouped with an upper weight between 0.5-3.5%
  • Top Size Decile Weights: Maximum 15% less than the stock universe

7. The Final Index is a blend of the two sub-portfolios, with each sub-portfolio weighted between 35-65%, with no month-to-month change over 15% permitted. Portfolio weights are based on trailing returns adjusted by volatility when returns are positive and trailing returns only when returns are negative.

Key Takeaways

It's a lot to absorb, but my key takeaways are that quality and growth exposure must be at least 15% above the market for both sub-portfolios. In addition, the individual security and sector constraints promote diversification, and taking into consideration a company's value features ensures limited exposure to the mega-cap growth stocks that dominate the major indices. As I'll touch on shortly, total overlap with the other ETFs is only 32-33%.

Performance Snapshot

Earlier, I provided QGRO's performance track record since my last review date against the following four peers:

  1. iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF)
  2. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)
  3. Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG)
  4. SPDR S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG)

With only $1.16 billion in assets under management, QGRO is tiny compared to these giants, but that doesn't mean it's inferior. Since its Index change on May 31, 2023, it's been the top performer in this peer group, gaining 39.16% compared to 34.73% for IWF, which is probably its closest peer. SCHG was second-best at 37.29%, followed by SPYG and VUG at 35.87% and 34.80%.

One drawback to QGRO is higher historical volatility, evidenced by its 18.30% annualized standard deviation figure from June 2023 through April 10, 2025. However, its downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio) were still the best.

QGRO Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between QGRO, IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG. Right away, you'll notice QGRO isn't nearly as tech-heavy as the other funds. Its 31.57% total exposure is 12.65% below the peer group average, with the offset roughly evenly distributed between Health Care (+5.23%) and Industrials (+5.82%).

Boston Scientific (BSX), QGRO's #11 holding, is the largest overweighted position in the Health Care sector, with a 2.34% allocation that's 2.04% above average. The Industrials sector is led by Uber Technologies (UBER) at #9, but what's most important is the minimal Magnificent Seven representation. Meta Platforms (META) and Netflix (NFLX) are there, but others, like Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), and Apple (AAPL), are #12, #15, and #23 on the list. QGRO's total Magnificent Seven exposure is only 11.60% compared to 49.00% on average for the other four funds.

QGRO Fundamentals: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for QGRO's top 25 holdings, which comprise 56.55% of the portfolio. Again, this demonstrates its excellent diversification features, as this concentration level is about 16% below the peer group average.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

QGRO: Strong Bottom-Line Growth Rates

QGRO's components have a weighted average 23.87% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate, which is about 1-2% better than the peer group. As QGRO's top stock, Booking Holdings (BKNG) is one example of an opportunity that's often ignored by growth ETF investors due to its relatively low market cap. Analysts expect 26.35% earnings growth over the next year, and due to its global operations, I believe it's well-positioned to defend against President Trump's tariff policies. Gross travel bookings grew 10% last year to $166 billion, and the company has resumed its steady growth rate that was halted in 2020 due to the global pandemic.

One drawback is that QGRO's three-year historical and one-year forward sales growth rates are a few percentage points below average, implying that its holdings must either cut costs or buy back shares at a faster rate. Still, using Seeking Alpha Growth Grades as a guide, I calculated a 6.21/10 sector-adjusted growth score for QGRO, which ranks #20/77 in the large-cap growth category, per my April 2025 ETF Rankings. It's a great score for such a diversified fund, and I attribute this to how American Century calculates growth using both historical and expected cash flow growth. On that note, I also calculated three-year historical free cash flow growth rates for each fund, which puts QGRO way ahead of the competition, as follows:

  • QGRO: 18.44%
  • IWF: 13.30%
  • SCHG: 12.99%
  • VUG: 12.84%
  • SPYG: 14.34%

QGRO: Quality Is Solid But Not As Good As Peers

Many analysts overlook the cost of diversification with regard to quality. For example, while the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) is obviously far more diversified than the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), its net margins are also about 7% lower (14.10% vs. 21.47%), so that's going to offset some of the safety benefits an equal-weight strategy offers. We must remember that 24/25 of SPY's top holdings have perfect "A+" Seeking Alpha Profitability Grades, so if we're going to deviate from that, we should demand a superior factor mix.

Based on my calculations, there's no doubt that QGRO is a lower-quality fund compared to IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG. I listed a few quality statistics above, like QGRO's components' 16.91% return on total capital and 19.70% free cash flow margins, but to complete the picture, please consider these additional metrics that indicate QGRO is below average almost across the board. The one bright spot is a slightly lower debt-equity ratio, but debt as a percentage of free cash flow is relatively high, and its components' interest coverage ratio is 8.63 points below average, so I don't think it has any real quality advantage worth touting.

We know QGRO's quality is below average, but the question is if it's fair for its size. To find out, I calculated sector-adjusted profit scores for other large-cap growth ETFs with market caps between $100 and $500 billion and found the following 13:

  1. Pacer Nasdaq 100 Top 50 Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (QQQG): 9.52/10
  2. Pacer US Large Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (COWG): 9.32/10
  3. VictoryShares Free Cash Flow Growth ETF (GFLW): 9.28/10
  4. American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO): 9.21/10
  5. VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat Growth ETF (MGRO): 9.19/10
  6. First Trust Growth Strength ETF (FTGS): 9.10/10
  7. Direxion NASDAQ-100 Equal Weighted ETF (QQQE): 9.00/10
  8. First Trust NASDAQ-100 Equal Weight ETF (QQEW): 9.00/10
  9. Invesco S&P 500 Pure Growth ETF (RPG): 8.99/10
  10. Invesco S&P 500 GARP ETF (SPGP): 8.90/10
  11. FT Raymond James Multicap Growth Equity ETF (RJMG): 8.62/10
  12. Sterling Capital Focus Equity ETF (LCG): 8.44/10
  13. First Trust Large Cap Growth AlphaDEX ETF (FTC): 8.33/10

QGRO's 9.21/10 profit score ranks #4/13, and it's worth noting that it also ranks #2/13 on diversification. As I've stated before, it's easy to design a high-quality fund with only 50 stocks, like with QQQG. However, it's much harder to maintain quality when you hold 189 stocks, like with QGRO.

Overall, I think QGRO's quality is adequate but definitely up for debate. I can see readers wanting to combine a more established fund like SCHG with a less-diversified but higher-quality fund with low overlap like QQQG. As shown below in the ETF Overlap Matrix I updated on April 1, QQQG and SCHG overlap by only 21.21%. It's a more efficient complement than QGRO (32.31%) and far more efficient than just combining funds like IWF/SCHG/VUG/SPYG, as their overlap is around 75-80%.

QGRO: "Stable Growth" Value Screens Help

QGRO's "Stable Growth" sub-portfolio maximizes the value and profitability composite scores, subject to some constraints covering sector, quality, growth, and market cap. We see this show up in QGRO's valuation ratios, which are superior to its peers across the board. For example, it trades at 23.81x forward earnings and 19.05x trailing cash flow, which are about 5% and 17% less than the peer group average on a relative basis.

In my view, it's nice to keep valuation in mind, as it could provide some protection in market downturns. QGRO's new strategy wasn't tested in 2022, but I imagine it would have outperformed by a few percentage points as high P/E stocks were targeted most. Furthermore, with recession fears increasing, getting your portfolio's P/E ratio down a little bit makes sense.

Investment Recommendation

In summary, QGRO is a large-cap growth fund that sacrifices some quality in exchange for greater exposure to the diversification, growth, and value factors. Its overlap with popular funds like IWF, SCHG, VUG, and SPYG is low, so even if you aren't ready to give those up, adding QGRO is a quick way to offset some of the concentration risks you might have.

I believe other analysts have underestimated QGRO's strengths and have relied primarily on its mediocre historical performance to justify their conclusions. What they may not realize is that QGRO's strategy changed two years ago, and it has been a top-performing fund in its category ever since, without much help from mega-cap growth stocks. I think it's the equivalent of the JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA) for the large-cap growth category, and I expect it will continue delivering solid results moving forward. Therefore, I'm reiterating my "buy" rating on QGRO, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments below.

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class (OAKBX) returned 0.91% versus -1.45% for the 60% S&P 500 / 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index for the quarter, and 9.51% since inception, versus 7.97% for the benchmark over the same period.
  • The equity portfolio returned -0.25% 1 for the quarter versus -4.27% for the S&P 500 Index (SP500,SPX). The fixed income portfolio returned 2.91%1 versus 2.78% for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The Fund allocation was 58.1% in equities, 41.1% in fixed income, and 0.8% cash.
  • At the sector level for the equity portfolio, the largest contributors to performance were financials and consumer staples, while consumer discretionary and communication services were the largest detractors.
  • As we have done in other periods when stock prices declined significantly, we are taking advantage of the opportunity to reposition the portfolio toward companies with the most attractive risk-adjusted returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

American International Group (AIG) was the top contributor during the quarter. The company reported improved premium growth, strong underwriting results, and significant capital return during the period. We expect these trends to continue, which has the potential to lead to strong EPS (earnings per share) growth and improved returns. Over time, we expect the company’s return on equity to converge with peer levels. As returns continue to improve, we expect the valuation discount to the industry’s top peers to narrow.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Molina Healthcare is a leading managed care company. Molina is the fourth largest player in managed Medicaid but has consistently delivered industry-leading growth and margins. In our view, this is thanks to the company’s exceptional management team and culture of operational excellence. We think Molina has a long runway for growth via continued share gains in Medicaid and untapped opportunities in their Medicare and Marketplace business segments. Recently, fundamentals have been pressured by an unprecedented redeterminations cycle in Medicaid, and valuations have compressed due to uncertainty around policy changes in a new political administration. We believe earnings pressure from redeterminations is temporary and that any Medicaid policy changes will prove manageable, providing us an opportunity to purchase shares at a meaningful discount to intrinsic value.

The S&P 500 Index is a float-adjusted, capitalization-weighted index of 500 U.S. large-capitalization stocks representing all major industries. It is a widely recognized index of broad, U.S. equity market performance. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), assetbacked securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities (agency and non-agency). These indexes are unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in them

The Oakmark Equity and Income Fund invests in mediumand lower-quality debt securities that have higher yield potential but present greater investment and credit risk than higher-quality securities. These risks may result in greater share price volatility. An economic downturn could severely disrupt the market in medium or lower grade debt securities and adversely affect the value of outstanding bonds and the ability of the issuers to repay principal and interest.

The Oakmark Equity and Income Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

The stocks of medium-sized companies tend to be more volatile than those of large companies and have underperformed the stocks of small and large companies during some periods.

All information provided is as of 03/31/2025 unless otherwise specified.
